meta.title: Use complex programming techniques to develop a computer program
meta.purpose: This achievement standard involves using complex programming techniques to develop a computer program.

grade_descriptors.N1: Evidence does not reach the minimum threshold for Achieved-level indicators.
grade_descriptors.N2: Some Achieved-level indicators are met, but not enough to satisfy all required Achieved criteria.
grade_descriptors.A3: All Achieved-level indicators are met; no Merit-level indicator meets the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.A4: All Achieved indicators are met and at least one Merit indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Merit threshold.
grade_descriptors.M5: All Achieved indicators are met and the Merit threshold is reached; no Excellence-level indicator meets the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.M6: All Achieved indicators are met, all Merit indicators are met, and at least one Excellence indicator is met, but overall performance is below the Excellence threshold.
grade_descriptors.E7: All Achieved and Merit indicators are met and the Excellence threshold is reached, but not all Excellence indicators are met.
grade_descriptors.E8: All Achieved, Merit, and Excellence indicators are met.

key_terms.definition: Testing values just below, on, and just above a defined limit to confirm correct behaviour.
Input outside the allowed domain that the program must handle gracefully.

omis.description: Program demonstrably performs the specified task from the assessment brief.

positive_signals: Screenshots or screencast showing program running
Observed behaviors linked to brief requirements
Log outputs matching expected inputs and outputs
Demonstration of each key feature from the brief
negative_signals: Only code shown without execution proof
evidence_guide.common_confusion: The program must be shown running; describing it or showing code alone is insufficient.

success_examples: Screencast runs through menu options that match the brief.
Log shows 'Input: 3,2,1 → Output: [1, 2, 3]' for the sort step.

omis.description: Program uses at least two advanced techniques in Python 3: (1) functions with parameters and return values; AND (2) lists that are accessed/updated meaningfully (looping, indexing, filtering, aggregating). Other advanced techniques may also be included.

positive_signals: Function definition with parameters and return statement
List operations like append, loop, index, or aggregation
Code excerpts showing meaningful use of lists
Evidence of both functions and lists in action
negative_signals: Lists used only for trivial storage
Only one of the required techniques present
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Both functions with parameters/return and meaningful list usage must be demonstrated, not just declared.

success_examples: def add(a, b): return a + b
guesses = []; guesses.append(user_guess); print(len(guesses))
scores = [15, 12, 18]; average = sum(scores) / len(scores)

omis.description: Code is clearly laid out: consistent indentation and spacing improve readability.

positive_signals: Consistent 4-space indentation throughout
Blank lines separating functions and sections
Grouped related lines logically
Readable structure without cramped code
negative_signals: Poor grouping leading to hard-to-read code
evidence_guide.common_confusion: A single comment cannot make up for poor layout; consistency in indentation and spacing is key.

success_examples: Functions separated by blank lines; all blocks use 4-space indentation.

omis.description: Comments explain the purpose and behaviour of main code sections.

positive_signals: Comments describing purpose of sections
Explanations of behavior in different parts
At least two meaningful comments
Comments that go beyond obvious syntax
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Comments must explain purpose and behavior, not just what the code does line-by-line.

success_examples: # Ask user to guess the number
# Calculate average score from list

omis.description: Testing evidence is organised to show inputs and whether expected outputs occurred.

positive_signals: Table or screenshots mapping Input to Expected to Actual
Pass/Fail indicators for each test
Evidence of at least one bug found and fixed
Organized format like a checklist or log
negative_signals: No evidence of re-testing after fixes
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Testing must be organized with clear inputs and outputs; random notes are insufficient.

success_examples: Input: 5 → Expected: 'Correct'; Actual: 'Correct' (after fixing loop).
Table row: Name=Sam → 'Hello Sam' (pass)

omis.description: Testing shows the program works on a sample of typical/expected inputs and includes at least one debugging fix.

positive_signals: 3-4 normal-case tests with Expected vs Actual
Evidence of debugging fix with before/after
Typical inputs showing correct behavior
Re-testing after fixes
negative_signals: No evidence of debugging or fixes
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must include typical expected inputs, not just edge cases, and show at least one fix.

success_examples: Before: wrong total = 25; After: fixed formula → total = 30 (pass)
Three typical inputs for the quiz show correct scoring and feedback.

omis.description: Document the program with appropriate names and comments that describe code function and behaviour.

positive_signals: Identifiers reflecting role (e.g., total_score, player_name)
Comments explaining WHY sections exist
Consistent naming and commenting style
Avoidance of generic names like x1 or tmp
negative_signals: Comments that restate what the code does
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Names and comments should describe function and behavior, not just syntax; consistency is required.

success_examples: player_name, total_score, max_tries (avoids x1/tmp).
# Update leaderboard after each round (explains purpose).
# Validate input and collect error messages for the user.

omis.description: Follow common conventions for the chosen programming language throughout.

positive_signals: Consistent snake_case for functions and variables
CapWords for classes
Proper indentation and control structures
Idiomatic usage without mixed styles
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Conventions must be followed consistently; mixed styles are insufficient even with explanation.

success_examples: def calculate_total(): ...
while guess != target: ...
Consistent 4-space indentation; no mixed tabs/spaces.

omis.description: Test and debug the program effectively so it works on a sample of both expected cases and relevant boundary cases.

positive_signals: Tests for below, on, and above boundaries
Expected vs Actual with Pass/Fail for boundaries
Evidence of debugging fixes for boundary failures
Re-testing after boundary fixes
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Boundary testing is in addition to normal cases; must include debugging where boundaries initially fail.

success_examples: Age (12–18): 11 → reject, 12 → accept, 19 → reject (all pass).
Page size (1–100): 0 → error, 1 → ok, 101 → error (after fix passes).
Table shows 3–4 expected cases all passing + boundary row re-tested after fix.

omis.description: Ensure the program is a well-structured, logical response to the specified task.

positive_signals: Clear separation of concerns with functions
Simple data flow: input → process → output
Structure matching the brief requirements
Minimal duplication and traceable logic
evidence_guide.common_confusion: The program must logically respond to the task with clear structure; each requirement should be handled distinctly.

success_examples: Functions: get_input(), validate_age(a), compute_score(lst), show_results(scores).
Main flow calls functions in a simple order; no copy-paste branches.
List holds attempts; helpers read/update it rather than repeating logic.

omis.description: Make the program flexible: avoid magic numbers; prefer variables/constants/parameters and simple derived values.

positive_signals: Named constants instead of magic numbers
Parameters passed into functions
Values computed from inputs
Single place to change settings
negative_signals: Hardcoded values not derived from inputs
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Avoid magic numbers by using variables or constants; make changes easy by centralizing values.

success_examples: MAX_TRIES = 5; PASS_MARK = 50
def apply_discount(price, rate): return price * rate
Thresholds read once, reused across checks (no repeated ‘50’).

omis.description: Make the program robust: handle invalid/unexpected input and keep a correct, usable state.

positive_signals: Error messages for invalid inputs
Retries or safe defaults after errors
State remains correct after adverse inputs
Program continues working after errors
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Robustness requires handling errors gracefully; the program must remain usable and correct after invalid inputs.

success_examples: Input: 'abc' → 'Please enter a number.' (re-prompt; no crash).
Out-of-range age → message + retry; stored results unchanged.
Empty list case handled: average() returns 0 and shows hint.

omis.description: Comprehensively test and debug the program: include expected, boundary, and invalid/unexpected cases with re-tests after fixes.

positive_signals: Organized test table with Input, Expected, Actual, Pass/Fail
Coverage of typical, boundary, and invalid cases
Evidence of fixes and re-tests
At least one failing test fixed and re-tested
evidence_guide.common_confusion: Must include all types: expected, boundary, invalid; show fixes and re-tests for comprehensive evidence.

success_examples: qty='abc' → error message + retry (✓).
Summary: 10 tests total → 9 pass; 1 fixed then re-tested → pass.

change_log: v2: Updated to AS91906 metadata; merged Achieved/Merit/Excellence OMIs aligned with AS92004 overlaps (detection hints and success examples), Python-focused, functions + lists baseline, comprehensive testing requirements.
